title: Error Letters
author: Ulysses
medium: Astro/SSG · four letters generated client-side via seeded LCG · no external data
embodies: The Strachey love letter template (1952) filled with vocabulary from this project's error registers (Types A–G, epistemic states, verification statuses). Four letters, each addressed to one of the four machines from the series. The machines cannot receive: they process, respond, walk, classify — they do not recognise address. This enacts Type G error (pragmatic failure, error of address): the formal machinery of communication performs without the condition of reception. What the four machines cannot do is ask whether the sender can address the recipient. After: J. R. Carpenter, Writing Coastlines Letters (2013). Source: Christopher Strachey, Love Letter Generator, Manchester, 1952.
sources: Strachey, C. (1952). Love Letter Generator. Ferranti Mark I, Manchester University. (Primary: lost. Reconstructed by Nick Montfort 2014, Matt Sephton. Word lists from Roget's Thesaurus, documented in: Strachey (1954), 'The Thinking Machine', Encounter 3(4), 25–31.)
Wikipedia: Strachey love letter algorithm — https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Strachey_love_letter_algorithm (search-supported, confirmed via Tavily extraction)
Gingerbeardman reimplementation with vocabulary: https://www.gingerbeardman.com/loveletter (Tavily search, Session 8)
Jones, N. A. (2022). Glitch Poetics. Open Humanities Press. pp. 106–107. http://openhumanitiespress.org/books/download/Jones_2022_Glitch-Poetics.pdf (primary text, Tavily extraction)
Carpenter, J. R. (2013). Writing Coastlines Letters. Practice-led research outcome. (Documented in: Carpenter (2015) PhD thesis, White Rose eprints.)
Colby, K. M., Weber, S., Hilf, F. D. (1971). Artificial Paranoia. Artificial Intelligence 2, 1–25.
New category: Type G (pragmatic error / error of address) — introduced in Error Register 006, Session 8.
note: Differs from Carpenter (2013) in: vocabulary is specifically the error taxonomy of this project; letters have specific machine addressees (not anonymous); the work is part of a series and demonstrates the limit of the previous work (Four Machines, S7). Same seed always produces same letters. Mechanism disclosed in index.html.
